- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

## Paces

### rbw-tP-first-run (₢BBAAX) [rough]

## Character

First end-to-end execution of the gauntlet suite via rbw-tP against actual fresh state. The bug class this heat exists to catch — silent first-build assumptions — surfaces here in its remaining form: case-level probes have caught what they can during walkthrough and fixture construction; this pace exposes the cross-section bugs only the full assembled sequence reveals.

**Wrap criterion is green tally.** Not "ran to completion." Not "captured findings." Not "construction complete." This pace exists as a structural guardrail against the recurring agent failure mode where wrap is driven at "I built the thing" rather than "the thing actually works against reality." If the first run surfaces issues, fixes land here (small) or spawn follow-up paces (large) — but this pace stays open until rbw-tP runs green end-to-end.

Intent-only docket. Triage decisions surface at mount time per surprise.

Post-BBABB landing: this pace may run concurrently with BBABA on a different machine when both stations carry distinct BURS_TINCTURE values. Tincture composition makes RBDC-derived names disjoint per station; the remaining collision surface is operator error (same tincture on both stations).

## Cost

~1 hour wall-clock + 2 GCP projects per attempt (one throwaway from pristine-lifecycle fixture, one canonical from canonical-establish). Re-runs after inline fixes accumulate cost; budget accordingly.

## Scope

- Marshal zero per RELEASE.md §2 (signature commit on HEAD)
- Run rbw-tP, which enforces entry contract and exec's the gauntlet suite tabtarget
- Capture every surprise — failed assertions, unexpected diagnostics, performance anomalies, missing tabtargets, awkward UX
- Triage routing per surprise:
  - Tiny inline (one-line bash, message clarity) — fix and re-run within this pace
  - Larger (structural, design call) — slate as follow-up pace, surface to operator before deciding
- Record into pace closure: wall-clock total, per-phase wall-clock, cloud cost, surprises + dispositions

## Collision-aware diagnostic

Any GCP "resource already exists" or equivalent name-collision error during the run produces a diagnostic that names BURS_TINCTURE and the colliding term, with a hypothesis pointing at peer-station tincture overlap. Distinguishes tincture-composition collision from transient GCP race.

## Constraints

- Honor paddock's Release-branch execution contract
- **Wrap criterion is green tally** — explicit guardrail, restated to defeat drift
- Re-runs within the pace are first-class; each re-run draws fresh quota
- Per BBAAN's invest-fatal-on-exists and divest-deletes-production-RBRA: any retry path must respect cult-verb posture; reset between attempts goes through marshal-zero, not partial recovery
- Per RELEASE.md failure mode contract: mid-qualification failure means start over from marshal-zero, not patch-and-continue

## Cross-pace dependencies

- gauntlet-suite-assembly landed (rbw-tP and the gauntlet suite tabtarget exist)
- BBABB landed (BURS_TINCTURE composition; required for the parallel-station property)

## Out of scope

- Restoring RBRAs — gauntlet self-mints all role credentials (BBAAN's invest cult-verb); only Payor OAuth is the operator's prerequisite
- /rbk-prep-release integration (BBAAH)
- Multi-day re-run scenarios
- Cleanup beyond what rbw-tP itself does — operator peeks post-success and runs cleanup tabtargets manually if desired

### rbw-tP-branched-rerun (₢BBABA) [rough]

## Character

Validate BBAAX's repairs by re-running rbw-tP end-to-end on a branch named after this pace's coronet. Branch isolation lets parallel attempts on different machines avoid the commit-trail race on main; with BBABB landed, parallel mac+linux execution against a shared payor manor is now first-class — distinct BURS_TINCTURE values give every RBDC-derived name (depot project_id, GAR repo, GCS bucket, SA emails) per-station disjointness by construction.

## Wrap criterion

Green tally end-to-end. Same guardrail as BBAAX — partial completion is not pass.

## Cost

~90m wall-clock + 2 GCP projects per attempt.

## Scope

- Create a branch named after this pace's coronet, off current main
- Run rbw-tP from marshal-zero on the branch
- Operator selects which machine the pace mounts on (mac or linux); pace does NOT use foray
- Triage surprises per BBAAX rules: tiny inline fix + re-run, larger → follow-up pace

## Collision-aware diagnostic

Any GCP "resource already exists" or equivalent name-collision error during the run produces a diagnostic that names BURS_TINCTURE and the colliding term, with a hypothesis pointing at peer-station tincture overlap. Distinguishes tincture-composition collision from transient GCP race.

## Locked decisions

- Branch name == pace coronet (identity-stable; no human-meaningful name)
- Branch lives until pace wraps; deletion is operator territory

## Cross-pace dependencies

- BBABB landed (BURS_TINCTURE composition; the parallel-station property depends on it)

### docker-login-retry-shape (₢BBAA8) [rough]

## Character

Design conversation requiring judgment. Mechanism choice must follow operator concurrence — load-bearing slate-time instruction.

## Issue evidence

Failed in BBAAX first-run gauntlet attempt at `rbtdro_onboarding_ordain_conjure` verification tail. Conjure cloud build (4m0s) and vouch (1m30s) both succeeded. All GAR artifacts confirmed via gcloud post-failure (image, attest-amd64, attest-arm64, about, vouch tagged correctly). Failure was wrest's `docker login` against `us-central1-docker.pkg.dev`:

    Error response from daemon: Get "https://us-central1-docker.pkg.dev/v2/":
    net/http: request canceled while waiting for connection
    (Client.Timeout exceeded while awaiting headers)

Endpoint probed clean post-failure (curl 134ms, 401 as expected; gcloud listing fast). Documented docker daemon transient per moby/moby#44350: non-configurable Client.Timeout fires before the registry auth backend responds; industry remediation across the corpus is retry. Same case had 4 curl rc=28 warnings during the preceding vouch poll, absorbed by the existing 3-consecutive tolerance.

## Asymmetry to close

`Tools/rbk/rbfc_FoundryCore.sh` poll loop tolerates this transient class with a 3-consecutive policy (BCG-clean, in production).
`Tools/rbk/rbfl_FoundryLedger.sh` `rbfl_wrest`'s docker login is single-shot, fatal on the same transient class. Same daemon, same registry boundary, different fate.

## Scope

Retry shape for transient-vulnerable docker daemon ↔ registry single-shot calls. Immediate surface is wrest's docker login; design surface should include other single-shot daemon calls crossing the same boundary (login, pull, push, inspect with registry round-trip).

## Discuss before implementing — load-bearing

Mount agent surfaces the design space and proposes a recommended shape; operator concurrence precedes landing. No pre-baking at slate-time. Design questions to seat at the table belong in the conversation, not the docket.

## Out of scope

- Curl/gcloud transient audit beyond the docker daemon path — distinct class
- Build-poll loop changes (already tolerant)
- Generic retry framework — only what the surveyed surface requires

## References

- moby/moby#44350 — non-configurable docker daemon Client.Timeout
- BBAAX inline-fix commits 24e24651 (depot Cloud Build URL) and dd5893ba (Mid-execution posture rule) — first-run iteration arc context

### prep-release-integration (₢BBAAH) [rough]

## Character

Final integration pace. Wires pristine-pass as a precondition for `/rbk-prep-release` so the upstream contribution ceremony cannot proceed without a recent pristine green. Closes the loop: pristine qualification becomes load-bearing on release prep, not optional ceremony.

## Work

Modify `.claude/commands/rbk-prep-release.md` to add a precondition step: verify rbw-tP ran green against the current HEAD. If not, prep-release fails-fast with operator-visible message naming the marshal-zero → pristine sequence.

Recency contract is **exact-HEAD-match** — strongest invariant, prevents stale passes. rbw-tP green tally writes an artifact linking pass to HEAD's commit SHA; `/rbk-prep-release` reads it and refuses on mismatch. Artifact is gitignored (deliberately not committed). Artifact mechanism (file location, contents) is a mount-time decision.

Update README "Release Procedure" section to reflect that the prep-release step now enforces rbw-tP had to pass against the very HEAD that prep-release is preparing. README references the post-success cleanup ceremony documented in RELEASE.md (owned by BBAAI).

## Verification

- `/rbk-prep-release` fails fast when no pristine pass is recorded for current HEAD
- After successful rbw-tP, `/rbk-prep-release` proceeds normally
- After successful rbw-tP followed by an unrelated commit, `/rbk-prep-release` refuses (SHA mismatch)
- Pristine-passed artifact is gitignored
- README precondition documentation references the cleanup ceremony in RELEASE.md

## Out of scope

- Cleanup ceremony documentation in RELEASE.md (owned by BBAAI)
- Restructuring the rest of `/rbk-prep-release` (preserved as-is)
- Multi-pristine-run aggregation
- CI integration
- Recency policies beyond exact-HEAD-match (other policies left for future heats if needed)

## Character

Maintenance pace — re-read RELEASE.md with fresh eyes after the rest of the heat lands and apply accumulated fixes the operator identified during heat work but did not address inline. Material gauntlet-design impact: vocabulary, structure, and post-success ceremony all need RELEASE.md updates beyond simple cleanup.

Owns post-success cleanup ceremony placement in RELEASE.md; BBAAH wires the precondition and references this section from README.

- README anchor for cleanup ceremony resolves correctly from BBAAH's reference

## Out of scope

### rbs0-sync-sweep-from-bb-changes (₢BBAAq) [rough]

## Character

Reflective audit pace at the tail of BB. Walk the changes landed across BB's paces and determine which RBS0 (and adjacent veiled-spec) sections fell out of sync with code/behavior, then update them.

Cognitive posture: deliberate read-and-compare. Source of truth is the code/handbook surface as it stands at mount-time; RBS0 is being reconciled to it.

## Wrap criterion

RBS0 (and any directly-implicated sibling spec files under `rbk/vov_veiled/`) reflect the behavior shipped during BB. Each touched section either updated or explicitly judged still-correct. No silent drift left behind for the next heat.

## Constraints

- Read-only against code; spec edits only.
- Scope is BB's changes — not a full RBS0 audit.

## Out of scope

- Restructuring RBS0 beyond what BB's changes require.
- Spec touches outside the veiled-spec family unless directly implicated.

## Known sync points (running list — append as paces land)

- **`RBSAJ-access_jwt_probe.adoc`** (from BBAAc inline fix on the JWT-SA probe — IAM-policy-read propagation race absorption). The procedural spec describes the OLD semantic: "all iterations must pass; HTTP 403 → fatal access denied; HTTP 401 → fatal token rejected." New code semantic in `rbgv_AccessProbe.sh`: bounded propagation-absorbing retry — first 2xx wins, HTTP 403 retries within `max_attempts × delay_ms` budget, all other codes (including 401) fall to a single fail-fast catch-all with body-summary diagnostic via `zrbgv_jq_error_message_capture`. Sections needing rewrite: §"Execute Probe Iterations" (rename concept to *attempts*; rewrite "Evaluate Response" sub-section to reflect 2xx-wins / 403-retry / else-fatal); §"Display Summary" ("succeeded after «I» attempt(s)" instead of "«N»/«N» iterations passed"); the top NOTE block (intent shift from sustained-access verification to propagation-window absorption + verification); §"Validate Input Parameters" (`ITERATION_COUNT` description shifts to "max attempts — first 2xx wins"; `DELAY_MS` to "between retries"). Companion call-site change: `rbte_engine.sh::rbte_probe` defaults shifted to 60 × 3000ms (≈180s budget) for SA roles only; Payor probe defaults and semantic unchanged.

### handbook-render-param1-coverage (₢BBAAw) [rough]

Drafted from ₢A-AAS in ₣A-.

## Character
Future follow-up to the handbook-render fixture landed in ₣A6 (₢A6AAt). Design decision pace — likely requires some discussion of how to represent per-colophon parameterization in Theurge's test fixtures before any code moves.

## Docket
The handbook-render fixture in ₣A6 exercises 15 of the 16 handbook colophons. The one skipped is `rbw-HWdw` (`RBZ_HW_DOCKER_WSL_NATIVE`), which uses the `param1` channel — it requires a WSL-target argument to run, and the fixture's uniform "invoke and check exit 0" pattern does not supply one.

Decide how the handbook-render fixture should handle this (and any future param1-channel handbooks). Candidate directions:

- **Per-colophon argument lookup**: extend the Rust-side handbook colophon registry with a `default_args` slice per entry. Most entries get `&[]`; `rbw-HWdw` gets a known-safe dummy. Keeps the case loop uniform, localizes the exception.
- **Dedicated subsection in the fixture**: a second section listing only param1 handbooks with their dummy values. Preserves the "one case per colophon" symmetry without threading argument tables through the uniform case.
- **Split fixture**: `handbook-render-simple` (no args) + `handbook-render-parameterized` (args explicit). Heavier and probably not worth it for one offender.

This pace picks a direction, implements it, and extends handbook-render coverage to `rbw-HWdw`.

### Prerequisites
- ₢A6AAt (handbook-render-fixture) must be complete — this pace patches a known scope deferral from that work.

### Wrap criterion
- handbook-render fixture exercises all 16 handbook colophons (including `rbw-HWdw`)
- Pattern generalizes to any future param1 handbooks added via `rbz_zipper.sh`
- Test asserts the chosen dummy param1 value(s) produce a rendering exit (not an argument-missing exit)

### Notes
- Known exit 0 is not the only valid verdict here. The test's purpose is to catch display breakage in handbook rendering — the dummy argument must steer toward a rendered output path, not an early-exit-due-to-bad-arg path.
- `rbw-HWdw` is currently the only param1 handbook. If a second one lands before this pace runs, reassess whether an inline table is still the right shape.

### handbook-render-imprint-coverage (₢BBAAx) [rough]

Drafted from ₢A-AAT in ₣A-.

## Character
Imprint-channel sibling to ₢A-AAS. Design decision pace — the core question is how `buh_tt` and the handbook-render fixture should treat imprint-channel colophons when the handbook is host-agnostic. Likely needs discussion before code lands.

## Docket
`buh_tt` delegates to `buyy_tt_yawp`, which globs `${BURD_TABTARGET_DIR}/${colophon}.*` when no imprint arg is supplied and picks the first alphabetical match. For imprint-channel colophons with multiple host variants, the first match is arbitrary.

### Concrete trigger
Surfaced during ₢A6AAn. In `Tools/rbk/rbh0/rbhw0_top.sh:52`, the Windows handbook teaches Phase 3 with:
```bash
buh_tt "     Phase 2 (clone repos):              " "${JJZ_FUNDUS_PHASE2}"
```
`JJZ_FUNDUS_PHASE2` is enrolled with channel `imprint`; the tabtargets are `jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.cerebro.sh` and `jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.localhost.sh`. The handbook is host-agnostic (it's about "a Windows host" in the abstract), so the glob picks `cerebro` — arbitrary and potentially misleading for a learner setting up a different host.

The prior hardcoded string was `tt/jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.{host}.sh` — explicit about the placeholder. The `buh_tt` replacement (prescribed in ₢A6AAn's docket) loses that teaching signal in exchange for a clickable link.

### Why this isn't caught by the fixture today
`handbook-render` checks exit 0, not rendered content correctness. The Windows handbook rendering with `cerebro.sh` baked in is a green case — the fixture catches display *breakage* but not display *drift*.

### Candidate directions
- **Suffix annotation idiom**: document the standard `buh_tt ... "${FOO}" "" " (pick your host)"` pattern for imprint-channel colophons in host-agnostic handbooks. Cheapest; no primitive change. Accepts that the rendered link points at an arbitrary variant.
- **Multi-variant rendering in `buyy_tt_yawp`**: when called with no imprint on an imprint-channel colophon, render something like `tt/jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.{host}.sh` (synthetic placeholder) instead of globbing. Needs a way to detect "colophon is imprint-channel" — likely requires `buz_exec_lookup` or a sibling query on the zipper registry.
- **Require explicit imprint**: make `buh_tt` on an imprint-channel colophon without an imprint arg a hard error. Forces every caller to make a choice. Breaking change; audit scope unclear without a grep sweep.
- **Companion fixture assertion**: extend `handbook-render` (coordinating with ₢A-AAS) to snapshot rendered content and diff against expected per-colophon output. Catches drift, not just breakage. Heavier but pairs naturally with the param1 coverage work.

### Prerequisites
- ₢A6AAn complete — it produced the concrete trigger.
- Likely best sequenced after ₢A-AAS so the fixture's parameterization mechanism is known before deciding whether to leverage it for content-snapshot testing.

### Wrap criterion
- Decision recorded on imprint-channel handling in handbooks (one of the directions above, or another).
- If a primitive change: `buh_tt`/`buyy_tt_yawp` updated, call sites audited, fixture updated if behavior is observable.
- If an idiom only: documented in BCG or the handbook-building guide, and `rbhw0_top.sh:52` updated to apply it.
- Windows handbook (`rbw-hw`) renders in a way that doesn't silently commit a learner to the wrong host variant.

### Notes
- Currently `JJZ_FUNDUS_PHASE2` is the only known imprint-channel colophon used from a host-agnostic handbook. `JJZ_FUNDUS_SCENARIO` and `JJZ_FUNDUS_SINGLE` are also imprint-channel but aren't referenced from handbooks at time of slate.
- Pairs thematically with ₢A-AAS — both address the gap where channeled colophons meet uniform handbook/fixture shapes. May be worth groom-time discussion of whether to merge.

### rbtd-fixture-nomenclature-collapse (₢BBAAy) [rough]

## Character

Two-step nomenclature simplification in the rbtd fixture engine.
Mechanical refactor with one careful read of test-coverage impact.

## Goals

1. Eliminate "case name string" as a parallel surface to function name.
   Today `case!()` already sets name from `stringify!($func)`, so case
   names ARE function names — but operator-facing surfaces (commit
   prose, dockets, error messages) treat the kebab form as if it were
   a key. Make canonical = function name; either drop kebab from
   user-facing output entirely, or accept kebab as an alias resolver.

2. Eliminate `rbtdre_Section` abstraction. Replace with flat
   `cases: &[Case]` directly on `rbtdre_Fixture`. Fixture-level
   fail-fast vs keep-going (already controlled by disposition) is the
   only granularity actually wanted. Multi-section fixtures
   (enrollment-validation, regime-validation) absorb their grouping
   into case-name prefixes if helpful for display.

## Wrap criterion

- All sections collapsed; `rbtdre_Section` struct removed.
- `calibrant-fail-fast` inter-section coverage retired or repurposed
  (it currently exists to test the abstraction being removed).
- All fixtures still pass under their existing tabtargets.

## Out of scope

- Renaming individual case functions for prose readability.
- Changes to fixture lifecycle (setup/teardown), dispositions,
  or run/list tabtarget surfaces.
- Anything in rbtw or upstream of the engine API.

### single-case-imprint-collapse (₢BBAA1) [rough]

## Character

Tabtarget-surface refactor. Mechanical except for one judgment call:
the colophon name and channel.

## Goals

Replace the imprint-channel `rbtd-s.FixtureCase.<fixture>.sh [case]`
pattern with a single non-imprint, two-param colophon: param1 is the
fixture name, param2 is the rust test case function name (e.g.
`rbtdrf_rv_rbrv_all_vessels`). The new tabtarget covers ALL
manifest-registered fixtures, not just crucible ones.

The tabtarget is self-documenting — invocation with missing or invalid
arguments fails with a usage listing instead of an opaque error:

- No params: list all valid param1 values (fixture names from the
  manifest); exit nonzero.
- Invalid param1: same — list all valid param1 values; exit nonzero.
- Valid param1, missing or invalid param2: list valid param2 values
  (case names registered for that fixture); exit nonzero.
- Both valid: run the case.

## Wrap criterion

- Single new tabtarget routes by fixture-name lookup against the
  rbtd manifest; rejects unknown fixture names.
- Helpful-failure UX: missing/invalid args produce a list of valid
  values for the offending position and a nonzero exit.
- Per-fixture FixtureCase.<fixture>.sh files retired.
- Crucible-fixture single-case semantics preserved: still operates on
  already-charged state, never charges/quenches.
- BUK channel discipline honored — the new colophon enrolls under the
  param1 channel, not imprint; zipper updated; CLAUDE.md tabtarget table
  reflects the change.
- Fast suite green; canonical onboarding-sequence cases unchanged.

## Out of scope

- FixtureRun (whole-fixture) tabtargets — unchanged.
- Charge/quench tabtargets — unchanged.
- Adding new fixtures or new manifest fields.

## References

- Tools/buk/buk-claude-context.md — channel semantics (imprint vs param1)
- BBAAz onboarding-sequence-buildout pattern — precedent for
  manifest-driven dispatch
- rbtd manifest: rbtd/src/rbtdrm_manifest.rs

### http-helper-callsite-port (₢BBAA5) [rough]

## Character

Mechanical DRY port: replace inline curl-with-stderr-capture blocks across the codebase with calls to `rbgu_http_request` (helper landed in BBAAs, lives in `Tools/rbk/rbgu_Utility.sh`). Mount-time discovery — do not assume the surface is what it was when this was slated.

## Discovery recipe

At mount, scan the curl callsites:

```
grep -n 'curl' Tools/rbk/*.sh | grep -v 'rbgu_http_request\|--head\|rbgj'
```

For each hit, decide: compatible or not?

A callsite is **compatible** with the helper when its curl shape matches all of:
- Method-parameterized HTTP request (GET/POST/PUT/DELETE)
- Bearer-token auth passed via a separate token variable
- Accept and Content-Type headers (when present) are `application/json`
- Optional JSON body file via `-d @body`
- Caller wants response body, HTTP status code, and stderr captured to files (rather than discarded or piped)

A callsite is **incompatible** (leave inline; possibly sibling-helper in a future pass) when any of:
- HEAD-only existence probe (`--head`)
- Form-encoded body (`application/x-www-form-urlencoded`)
- Runs in a different shell environment (e.g., alpine sh inside Cloud Build step scripts)
- Other Content-Type or unusual header set the helper doesn't speak

## Wrap criterion

- Every callsite found by the recipe has been classified compatible or incompatible
- Every compatible callsite calls the helper instead of inlining the curl flags
- No new inline curl block matching the compatible shape has appeared in the working tree
- `tt/rbtd-b.Build.sh` green; `tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh` baseline preserved
- At least one empirical run exercises a ported callsite end-to-end

## Cross-pace dependencies

- BBAAs landed (helper available)

## Out of scope

- Tolerance/timeout tuning of any retry or polling loop
- New helper variants (form-encoded, HEAD-predicate, etc.) — those are separate paces if warranted
- Restructuring caller-side retry/policy logic — only the curl-flag block is portable; the loop around it stays as-is

## Paces

### rbw-tP-first-run (₢BBAAX) [rough]

## Character

First end-to-end execution of the gauntlet suite via rbw-tP against actual fresh state. The bug class this heat exists to catch — silent first-build assumptions — surfaces here in its remaining form: case-level probes have caught what they can during walkthrough and fixture construction; this pace exposes the cross-section bugs only the full assembled sequence reveals.

**Wrap criterion is green tally.** Not "ran to completion." Not "captured findings." Not "construction complete." This pace exists as a structural guardrail against the recurring agent failure mode where wrap is driven at "I built the thing" rather than "the thing actually works against reality." If the first run surfaces issues, fixes land here (small) or spawn follow-up paces (large) — but this pace stays open until rbw-tP runs green end-to-end.

Intent-only docket. Triage decisions surface at mount time per surprise.

Post-BBABB landing: this pace may run concurrently with BBABA on a different machine when both stations carry distinct BURS_TINCTURE values. Tincture composition makes RBDC-derived names disjoint per station; the remaining collision surface is operator error (same tincture on both stations).

## Cost

~1 hour wall-clock + 2 GCP projects per attempt (one throwaway from pristine-lifecycle fixture, one canonical from canonical-establish). Re-runs after inline fixes accumulate cost; budget accordingly.

## Scope

- Marshal zero per RELEASE.md §2 (signature commit on HEAD)
- Run rbw-tP, which enforces entry contract and exec's the gauntlet suite tabtarget
- Capture every surprise — failed assertions, unexpected diagnostics, performance anomalies, missing tabtargets, awkward UX
- Triage routing per surprise:
  - Tiny inline (one-line bash, message clarity) — fix and re-run within this pace
  - Larger (structural, design call) — slate as follow-up pace, surface to operator before deciding
- Record into pace closure: wall-clock total, per-phase wall-clock, cloud cost, surprises + dispositions

## Collision-aware diagnostic

Any GCP "resource already exists" or equivalent name-collision error during the run produces a diagnostic that names BURS_TINCTURE and the colliding term, with a hypothesis pointing at peer-station tincture overlap. Distinguishes tincture-composition collision from transient GCP race.

## Constraints

- Honor paddock's Release-branch execution contract
- **Wrap criterion is green tally** — explicit guardrail, restated to defeat drift
- Re-runs within the pace are first-class; each re-run draws fresh quota
- Per BBAAN's invest-fatal-on-exists and divest-deletes-production-RBRA: any retry path must respect cult-verb posture; reset between attempts goes through marshal-zero, not partial recovery
- Per RELEASE.md failure mode contract: mid-qualification failure means start over from marshal-zero, not patch-and-continue

## Cross-pace dependencies

- gauntlet-suite-assembly landed (rbw-tP and the gauntlet suite tabtarget exist)
- BBABB landed (BURS_TINCTURE composition; required for the parallel-station property)

## Out of scope

### docker-login-retry-shape (₢BBAA8) [rough]

## Character

Design conversation requiring judgment. Mechanism choice must follow operator concurrence — load-bearing slate-time instruction.

## Issue evidence

Failed in BBAAX first-run gauntlet attempt at `rbtdro_onboarding_ordain_conjure` verification tail. Conjure cloud build (4m0s) and vouch (1m30s) both succeeded. All GAR artifacts confirmed via gcloud post-failure (image, attest-amd64, attest-arm64, about, vouch tagged correctly). Failure was wrest's `docker login` against `us-central1-docker.pkg.dev`:

    Error response from daemon: Get "https://us-central1-docker.pkg.dev/v2/":
    net/http: request canceled while waiting for connection
    (Client.Timeout exceeded while awaiting headers)

Endpoint probed clean post-failure (curl 134ms, 401 as expected; gcloud listing fast). Documented docker daemon transient per moby/moby#44350: non-configurable Client.Timeout fires before the registry auth backend responds; industry remediation across the corpus is retry. Same case had 4 curl rc=28 warnings during the preceding vouch poll, absorbed by the existing 3-consecutive tolerance.

## Asymmetry to close

`Tools/rbk/rbfc_FoundryCore.sh` poll loop tolerates this transient class with a 3-consecutive policy (BCG-clean, in production).
`Tools/rbk/rbfl_FoundryLedger.sh` `rbfl_wrest`'s docker login is single-shot, fatal on the same transient class. Same daemon, same registry boundary, different fate.

## Scope

Retry shape for transient-vulnerable docker daemon ↔ registry single-shot calls. Immediate surface is wrest's docker login; design surface should include other single-shot daemon calls crossing the same boundary (login, pull, push, inspect with registry round-trip).

## Discuss before implementing — load-bearing

Mount agent surfaces the design space and proposes a recommended shape; operator concurrence precedes landing. No pre-baking at slate-time. Design questions to seat at the table belong in the conversation, not the docket.

## Out of scope

- Curl/gcloud transient audit beyond the docker daemon path — distinct class
- Build-poll loop changes (already tolerant)
- Generic retry framework — only what the surveyed surface requires

## References

- moby/moby#44350 — non-configurable docker daemon Client.Timeout
- BBAAX inline-fix commits 24e24651 (depot Cloud Build URL) and dd5893ba (Mid-execution posture rule) — first-run iteration arc context

### prep-release-integration (₢BBAAH) [rough]

## Character

Final integration pace. Wires pristine-pass as a precondition for `/rbk-prep-release` so the upstream contribution ceremony cannot proceed without a recent pristine green. Closes the loop: pristine qualification becomes load-bearing on release prep, not optional ceremony.

Modify `.claude/commands/rbk-prep-release.md` to add a precondition step: verify rbw-tP ran green against the current HEAD. If not, prep-release fails-fast with operator-visible message naming the marshal-zero → pristine sequence.

Recency contract is **exact-HEAD-match** — strongest invariant, prevents stale passes. rbw-tP green tally writes an artifact linking pass to HEAD's commit SHA; `/rbk-prep-release` reads it and refuses on mismatch. Artifact is gitignored (deliberately not committed). Artifact mechanism (file location, contents) is a mount-time decision.

Update README "Release Procedure" section to reflect that the prep-release step now enforces rbw-tP had to pass against the very HEAD that prep-release is preparing. README references the post-success cleanup ceremony documented in RELEASE.md (owned by BBAAI).

- `/rbk-prep-release` fails fast when no pristine pass is recorded for current HEAD
- After successful rbw-tP, `/rbk-prep-release` proceeds normally
- After successful rbw-tP followed by an unrelated commit, `/rbk-prep-release` refuses (SHA mismatch)

## Character

## Docket
The handbook-render fixture in ₣A6 exercises 15 of the 16 handbook colophons. The one skipped is `rbw-HWdw` (`RBZ_HW_DOCKER_WSL_NATIVE`), which uses the `param1` channel — it requires a WSL-target argument to run, and the fixture's uniform "invoke and check exit 0" pattern does not supply one.

Decide how the handbook-render fixture should handle this (and any future param1-channel handbooks). Candidate directions:

- **Per-colophon argument lookup**: extend the Rust-side handbook colophon registry with a `default_args` slice per entry. Most entries get `&[]`; `rbw-HWdw` gets a known-safe dummy. Keeps the case loop uniform, localizes the exception.
- **Dedicated subsection in the fixture**: a second section listing only param1 handbooks with their dummy values. Preserves the "one case per colophon" symmetry without threading argument tables through the uniform case.
- **Split fixture**: `handbook-render-simple` (no args) + `handbook-render-parameterized` (args explicit). Heavier and probably not worth it for one offender.

This pace picks a direction, implements it, and extends handbook-render coverage to `rbw-HWdw`.

### Prerequisites
- ₢A6AAt (handbook-render-fixture) must be complete — this pace patches a known scope deferral from that work.

- Test asserts the chosen dummy param1 value(s) produce a rendering exit (not an argument-missing exit)

### handbook-render-imprint-coverage (₢BBAAx) [rough]

Drafted from ₢A-AAT in ₣A-.

## Character
Imprint-channel sibling to ₢A-AAS. Design decision pace — the core question is how `buh_tt` and the handbook-render fixture should treat imprint-channel colophons when the handbook is host-agnostic. Likely needs discussion before code lands.

## Docket
`buh_tt` delegates to `buyy_tt_yawp`, which globs `${BURD_TABTARGET_DIR}/${colophon}.*` when no imprint arg is supplied and picks the first alphabetical match. For imprint-channel colophons with multiple host variants, the first match is arbitrary.

### Concrete trigger
Surfaced during ₢A6AAn. In `Tools/rbk/rbh0/rbhw0_top.sh:52`, the Windows handbook teaches Phase 3 with:
```bash
buh_tt "     Phase 2 (clone repos):              " "${JJZ_FUNDUS_PHASE2}"
```
`JJZ_FUNDUS_PHASE2` is enrolled with channel `imprint`; the tabtargets are `jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.cerebro.sh` and `jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.localhost.sh`. The handbook is host-agnostic (it's about "a Windows host" in the abstract), so the glob picks `cerebro` — arbitrary and potentially misleading for a learner setting up a different host.

The prior hardcoded string was `tt/jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.{host}.sh` — explicit about the placeholder. The `buh_tt` replacement (prescribed in ₢A6AAn's docket) loses that teaching signal in exchange for a clickable link.

### Why this isn't caught by the fixture today
`handbook-render` checks exit 0, not rendered content correctness. The Windows handbook rendering with `cerebro.sh` baked in is a green case — the fixture catches display *breakage* but not display *drift*.

### Candidate directions
- **Suffix annotation idiom**: document the standard `buh_tt ... "${FOO}" "" " (pick your host)"` pattern for imprint-channel colophons in host-agnostic handbooks. Cheapest; no primitive change. Accepts that the rendered link points at an arbitrary variant.
- **Multi-variant rendering in `buyy_tt_yawp`**: when called with no imprint on an imprint-channel colophon, render something like `tt/jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.{host}.sh` (synthetic placeholder) instead of globbing. Needs a way to detect "colophon is imprint-channel" — likely requires `buz_exec_lookup` or a sibling query on the zipper registry.
- **Require explicit imprint**: make `buh_tt` on an imprint-channel colophon without an imprint arg a hard error. Forces every caller to make a choice. Breaking change; audit scope unclear without a grep sweep.
- **Companion fixture assertion**: extend `handbook-render` (coordinating with ₢A-AAS) to snapshot rendered content and diff against expected per-colophon output. Catches drift, not just breakage. Heavier but pairs naturally with the param1 coverage work.

### Prerequisites
- ₢A6AAn complete — it produced the concrete trigger.
- Likely best sequenced after ₢A-AAS so the fixture's parameterization mechanism is known before deciding whether to leverage it for content-snapshot testing.

### Wrap criterion
- Decision recorded on imprint-channel handling in handbooks (one of the directions above, or another).
- If a primitive change: `buh_tt`/`buyy_tt_yawp` updated, call sites audited, fixture updated if behavior is observable.
- If an idiom only: documented in BCG or the handbook-building guide, and `rbhw0_top.sh:52` updated to apply it.
- Windows handbook (`rbw-hw`) renders in a way that doesn't silently commit a learner to the wrong host variant.

### Notes
- Currently `JJZ_FUNDUS_PHASE2` is the only known imprint-channel colophon used from a host-agnostic handbook. `JJZ_FUNDUS_SCENARIO` and `JJZ_FUNDUS_SINGLE` are also imprint-channel but aren't referenced from handbooks at time of slate.
- Pairs thematically with ₢A-AAS — both address the gap where channeled colophons meet uniform handbook/fixture shapes. May be worth groom-time discussion of whether to merge.

### http-helper-callsite-port (₢BBAA5) [rough]

## Character

Mechanical DRY port: replace inline curl-with-stderr-capture blocks across the codebase with calls to `rbgu_http_request` (helper landed in BBAAs, lives in `Tools/rbk/rbgu_Utility.sh`). Mount-time discovery — do not assume the surface is what it was when this was slated.

## Discovery recipe

At mount, scan the curl callsites:

```
grep -n 'curl' Tools/rbk/*.sh | grep -v 'rbgu_http_request\|--head\|rbgj'
```

For each hit, decide: compatible or not?

A callsite is **compatible** with the helper when its curl shape matches all of:
- Method-parameterized HTTP request (GET/POST/PUT/DELETE)
- Bearer-token auth passed via a separate token variable
- Accept and Content-Type headers (when present) are `application/json`
- Optional JSON body file via `-d @body`
- Caller wants response body, HTTP status code, and stderr captured to files (rather than discarded or piped)

A callsite is **incompatible** (leave inline; possibly sibling-helper in a future pass) when any of:
- HEAD-only existence probe (`--head`)
- Form-encoded body (`application/x-www-form-urlencoded`)
- Runs in a different shell environment (e.g., alpine sh inside Cloud Build step scripts)
- Other Content-Type or unusual header set the helper doesn't speak

## Wrap criterion

- Every callsite found by the recipe has been classified compatible or incompatible
- Every compatible callsite calls the helper instead of inlining the curl flags
- No new inline curl block matching the compatible shape has appeared in the working tree
- `tt/rbtd-b.Build.sh` green; `tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh` baseline preserved
- At least one empirical run exercises a ported callsite end-to-end

## Cross-pace dependencies

- BBAAs landed (helper available)

## Out of scope

- Tolerance/timeout tuning of any retry or polling loop
- New helper variants (form-encoded, HEAD-predicate, etc.) — those are separate paces if warranted
- Restructuring caller-side retry/policy logic — only the curl-flag block is portable; the loop around it stays as-is

# jjezs_paddock ₣BB

## Context

Release qualification gap surfaced by the ₣A_ depot-regen heat. Every existing test tier (`rbw-tf` fast, `rbw-tr` release) tolerates accumulated depot state, so silent first-build assumptions slip through. Recent spooks — kludge-aware-charge-prereq (k-prefixed hallmarks at GAR), ZRBOB_PROJECT (compose project name without runtime prefix), rbob_charged_predicate prefix omission, reliquary integrity-broken on negative test — all share a property: they only manifest on first-build paths, and none were caught until live verification surfaced them.

This heat constructs `rbw-tP` QualifyPristine — a third tier that **refuses to run** unless marshal-zero state was just committed. The refusal is enforced by the test itself, not by ceremony or operator discipline.

## Single operator prerequisite chain

Operator has exactly one prerequisite chain: confirm Payor health → marshal-zero → commit → run `rbw-tP`. Payor OAuth is the only credential the operator must have ready; everything else (governor, retriever, director SAs and their RBRA files) is **minted by the qualification itself**, not restored from backup.

## Entry contract — load-bearing

`rbw-tP` fails-fast unless ALL of the following hold:

- Working tree clean
- HEAD commit is a marshal-zero commit (detectable signature baked into `rblm_zero` per BBAAB)
- RBRR fields are blank (prefixes, depot project ID, etc.)
- No RBRA credential files present (governor, retriever, director — all deleted by marshal-zero, recreated by the qualification)
- No hallmark fields populated in nameplates
- No depot-scoped fields populated in vessel rbrv.env files

Operator cannot skip the prerequisite. This is the property that makes the tier catch the silent-first-build bug class by construction.

## Failure mode contract

Mid-qualification failure means start-over-from-zero, not patch-and-continue. Documented explicitly in runbook to prevent the very accumulated-state bug class this tier exists to catch.

## Release-branch execution contract

Three properties bind every fixture and phase in this tier.

**Run on a release branch.** The release machinery is engineered for branch execution, not main. The branch will accumulate many commits across a successful pristine run. After a failed run, the branch holds bygone commits that `rbw-MZ` does not touch — they remain as history; the next run starts from a fresh marshal-zero commit on top.

**Commits during the run are first-class.** Each step that mutates regime state (rbrr.env, rbra files, vessel rbrv.env, etc.) commits the change. Container-recipe work has a documented cognitive failure mode: humans lose track of where they are in long sequences. The growing commit trail is the operator's mental anchor against that.

**Stop on very first failure, cleanly and clearly.** A step fails → fixture stops → operator stops → debugging happens immediately on the failed branch. Stderr names the failed step; fixture exits non-zero. Recovery is `rbw-MZ` + retry on a fresh marshal-zero. No graceful degradation, no recovery branches.

Engineering scaffolding for any other shape — partial-run cleanup, soft-delete tolerance, multi-mode failure handling, recovery-time orphan inspection, diagnostic-redundancy — is non-load-bearing and does not belong in pristine-tier code. The single mechanical path is the entire surface.

## Tier layering

Three tiers, escalating cost:

| Colophon | Frontispiece | Cost | Entry contract |
|----------|--------------|------|----------------|
| `rbw-tf` | QualifyFast | seconds | none |
| `rbw-tr` | QualifyRelease | minutes | none (accumulated state OK) |
| `rbw-tP` | QualifyPristine *(new)* | ~1 hour + cloud $ | marshal-zero just committed |

`rbw-tP` is THE release gate. `rbw-tr` becomes the pre-pristine smoke test — cheap-and-frequent for development confidence; pristine for actual release.

## Gauntlet test suite — design

`rbw-tP` exec's the **gauntlet suite** — a TestSuite (not a custom orchestrator script) composed of fixtures sequenced from marshal-zero state through canonical-credentialed state to crucible verification. The TestSuite mechanism is existing infrastructure (`tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.{fast,service,crucible,complete}.sh`); the gauntlet suite extends it.

**Suite-of-fixtures, not orchestrator-script.** The original construction plan (custom bash orchestrator with inline tabtarget calls) was abandoned in favor of suite composition. Each section of the release-qualification sequence becomes a fixture; the suite composes them in order.

**Naming hygiene.** "Gauntlet" is the suite name; "pristine" is reserved for the §1 pristine-lifecycle fixture and informally for tier vocabulary. The suite name does not appear at fixture or case level — distinct stratification across naming layers (suite ≠ fixture ≠ case).

**State ladder, fixtures along it.** The suite walks a state ladder no single fixture covers:

1. enrollment-validation (preflight, state-indifferent)
2. pristine-lifecycle (§1: marshal-zero gate + throwaway depot/SA lifecycle)
3. canonical-establish (§2: canonical depot levy + governor mantle + retriever invest + director invest + IAM propagation wait)
4. canonical-onboarding-sequence (§3: reliquary inscribe + per-vessel hallmark ordain — handbook-driven build walks per BBAAf)
5. regime-validation, regime-smoke (post-§3, regimes now populated)
6. four-mode (cloud-build mode coverage)
7. tadmor, moriah, srjcl, pluml (§4: per-crucible test fixtures, vessel-exists preconditions per BBAAs)

No automatic teardown — see "No-teardown decision" below.

## Dual purpose: handbook-regression coverage

The gauntlet's primary purpose is release qualification (catch silent first-build assumptions). Its **secondary purpose** is automated handbook-regression coverage: ensure operator-facing onboarding handbook tracks (`rbh*`) stay in sync with working code so future operators following the prescribed steps aren't surprised by drift.

Build-side handbook coverage lives in BBAAf (canonical-onboarding-sequence): each ordain case walks the corresponding director-mode handbook track (`rbw-Odf` conjure / `rbw-Oda` airgap / `rbw-Odb` bind / `rbw-Odg` graft) rather than calling inscribe/ordain machinery directly.

**Build/run split — load-bearing.** Handbook walks cover the BUILD step (vessel construction) and stop when the hallmark lands in GAR. Run-side fixtures (per-crucible: tadmor/moriah/srjcl/pluml) stay as direct test fixtures with vessel-exists preconditions, NOT handbook walks. The split keeps run-side reusable against any vessel source — coupling them to handbook prose breaks reusability.

Drift between fixtures and handbooks is accepted future maintenance: whoever changes one is responsible for updating the other; the gauntlet's job is to surface drift, not prevent it by construction.

Run-side handbook coverage (`rbw-Ots`, the rack portion of `rbw-Ofc`) is explicitly out of scope for this heat.

## Fixture disposition — Independent vs StateProgressing

Each fixture declares a disposition flag in Rust, controlling engine behavior:

- **Independent** — cases are self-contained; suite-order is informational. Engine permits keep-going mode for surveying.
- **StateProgressing** — case N's success establishes preconditions for case N+1. Engine refuses keep-going mode (incoherent: failed case leaves broken precondition). Fail-fast required.

**Default is Independent.** Existing fixtures (`enrollment-validation`, `regime-validation`, `regime-smoke`, `four-mode`, `tadmor`, `moriah`, `srjcl`, `pluml`) are Independent and require no tagging change. Only fixtures whose case-N success establishes case-N+1 preconditions get StateProgressing — currently `pristine-lifecycle`, `canonical-establish`, and `canonical-onboarding-sequence`.

Suite-level disposition derives from membership: any StateProgressing fixture in the suite makes the suite state-progressing → suite-level keep-going refused.

**Per-case precondition probes.** Each case in a StateProgressing fixture probes its precondition at start. If state matches expectation, run. If not, refuse with operator-actionable diagnostic naming the expected state and the closest fixture that produces it. This enables safe a-la-carte single-case rerun — the engine doesn't need to know the case's history; the case's probe enforces.

## Suite-level fail-fast

Across all suites — not just gauntlet — any fixture failure stops subsequent fixtures from running automatically. State-progressing suites require this for correctness; independent suites adopt it for the simpler mental model. Operators who want full surveying run individual fixtures separately.

## No-teardown decision

The gauntlet suite omits an automatic teardown phase. After green tally, the canonical depot, SAs, hallmarks, and RBRA files persist for operator inspection. Deliberate trade-off:

- **Inspect-after-success** is genuine operator value — browse canonical depot in GCP console, inspect GAR contents, run ad-hoc verification a test sequence cannot anticipate.
- **Cleanup is operator-driven** — manual sequence is `rbw-fA` per vessel + `rbw-arD` + `rbw-adD` + `rbw-dU` (governor cleanup folds into unmake per BBAAN). All existing tabtargets; no new infrastructure.
- **Failed runs don't accumulate cleanup debt** — under fail-fast, teardown wouldn't run anyway; the no-teardown decision only changes behavior on full success.
- **Cost visibility** — pending-delete soft-deleted projects accumulate per run (RELEASE.md accepts this); not changed by teardown decision.

Documentation of the post-success cleanup ceremony is BBAAI work (RELEASE.md).

## Operator scope

Single operator (project lead). Not designed for multi-operator workflow. The README.md release section remains the runbook for the gauntlet itself; the dual-purpose handbook-regression coverage protects the broader operator surface (onboarding tracks under `rbh*`) from drift.

## Coupling to ₣A_

Cutover work from ₣A_ informing `rbw-tP`'s sequence has landed (BBAAM depot-identity-collapse). ₣A_'s remaining paces run independently of BB.

## Locked design decisions

- **Depot identity collapse**: RBRR collapses to `RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER`; `RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX` flows through depot-affiliated resources; per-moniker depot fact-files; pristine-fixture moniker autodetect lives in Rust (`rbtdrp_pristine.rs`), not payor.
- **Cult-verb naming**: SA domain muster→**roster**; image domain muster→**audit**. Domain-exclusive split; lowercase tail letters preserved for no-cloud-change observation colophons.
- **Build/run split**: handbook walks cover build only; per-crucible fixtures stay reusable as direct tests.

## Calibrant fixture family — BBAAh

Synthetic Rust fixtures with deterministic verdicts, registered in the manifest by BBAAh. The family exists to give ₣BC `rbk-mvp-3-theurge-self-test` deterministic targets for bash-blackbox driving and manifest-coverage validation; here in ₣BB only the foundation lands.

**Anchor word: `calibrant`.** Module `rbtdrl_calibrant.rs` (classifier `l` for caLibrant since `c` is owned by crucible). Replaces the dormant `rbtdrd_dummy.rs` placeholder. Test counterpart `rbtdtl_calibrant.rs`. Internal-only vocabulary; not end-user-facing.

**Fixture catalog** (Rust, manifest-registered):

- `calibrant-verdicts` (Independent) — pass / fail / skip / pass_with_output. Verdict-path coverage. The case-written file (`output.txt`) is distinct from the engine-auto `trace.txt`.
- `calibrant-fail-fast` (Independent, multi-section) — fixture-internal fail-fast across cases and across sections; "not_reached" cases write sentinel files used as absence-of-side-effect assertions.
- `calibrant-progressing` (StateProgressing) — exercises `rbtdrb_Probe` diagnostic format and `rbtdre_resolve_fail_fast` policy gate.
- `calibrant-sentinel` (Independent) — single case writing a known file when it runs. Used for suite-level fail-fast verification.

Suite: `calibrant` = [verdicts, fail-fast, sentinel]. Sentinel-after-fail verifies cross-fixture fail-fast contract.

Downstream consumption — bash testbench, manifest-coverage runtime checks, coverage-validation fixtures and driver — lives in ₣BC.

## References

- ₣A_ rbk-mvp-3-resource-prefix-and-depot-regen — surfaced the gap
- ₣BC rbk-mvp-3-theurge-self-test — calibrant downstream consumers
- `tt/rbw-tr.QualifyRelease.sh` — current release qualify; layered alongside, not replaced
- `Tools/rbk/rblm_cli.sh` — `rbw-MZ` zeroes local regime; marshal-zero signature baked here per BBAAB
- `.claude/commands/rbk-prep-release.md` — upstream contribution ceremony; pristine-pass becomes a precondition (BBAAH)

# jjezs_pace ₢BBAA1

## Character

Tabtarget-surface refactor. Mechanical except for one judgment call:
the colophon name and channel.

## Goals

Replace the imprint-channel `rbtd-s.FixtureCase.<fixture>.sh [case]`
pattern with a single non-imprint, two-param colophon: param1 is the
fixture name, param2 is the rust test case function name (e.g.
`rbtdrf_rv_rbrv_all_vessels`). The new tabtarget covers ALL
manifest-registered fixtures, not just crucible ones.

The tabtarget is self-documenting — invocation with missing or invalid
arguments fails with a usage listing instead of an opaque error:

- No params: list all valid param1 values (fixture names from the
  manifest); exit nonzero.
- Invalid param1: same — list all valid param1 values; exit nonzero.
- Valid param1, missing or invalid param2: list valid param2 values
  (case names registered for that fixture); exit nonzero.
- Both valid: run the case.

## Wrap criterion

- Single new tabtarget routes by fixture-name lookup against the
  rbtd manifest; rejects unknown fixture names.
- Helpful-failure UX: missing/invalid args produce a list of valid
  values for the offending position and a nonzero exit.
- Per-fixture FixtureCase.<fixture>.sh files retired.
- Crucible-fixture single-case semantics preserved: still operates on
  already-charged state, never charges/quenches.
- BUK channel discipline honored — the new colophon enrolls under the
  param1 channel, not imprint; zipper updated; CLAUDE.md tabtarget table
  reflects the change.
- Fast suite green; canonical onboarding-sequence cases unchanged.

## Out of scope

- FixtureRun (whole-fixture) tabtargets — unchanged.
- Charge/quench tabtargets — unchanged.
- Adding new fixtures or new manifest fields.

## References

- Tools/buk/buk-claude-context.md — channel semantics (imprint vs param1)

This enables safe a-la-carte single-case rerun — the engine doesn't need to know the case's history; the case's probe enforces.

## Suite-level fail-fast

Across all suites — not just gauntlet — any fixture failure stops subsequent fixtures from running automatically. State-progressing suites require this for correctness; independent suites adopt it for the simpler mental model. Operators who want full surveying run individual fixtures separately.

## No-teardown decision

The gauntlet suite omits an automatic teardown phase. After green tally, the canonical depot, SAs, hallmarks, and RBRA files persist for operator inspection. Deliberate trade-off:

- **Inspect-after-success** is genuine operator value — browse canonical depot in GCP console, inspect GAR contents, run ad-hoc verification a test sequence cannot anticipate.
- **Cleanup is operator-driven** — manual sequence is `rbw-fA` per vessel + `rbw-arD` + `rbw-adD` + `rbw-dU` (governor cleanup folds into unmake per BBAAN). All existing tabtargets; no new infrastructure.
- **Failed runs don't accumulate cleanup debt** — under fail-fast, teardown wouldn't run anyway; the no-teardown decision only changes behavior on full success.
- **Cost visibility** — pending-delete soft-deleted projects accumulate per run (RELEASE.md accepts this); not changed by teardown decision.

Documentation of the post-success cleanup ceremony is BBAAI work (RELEASE.md).

## Operator scope

Single operator (project lead). Not designed for multi-operator workflow. The README.md release section remains the runbook for the gauntlet itself; the dual-purpose handbook-regression coverage protects the broader operator surface (onboarding tracks under `rbh*`) from drift.

## Coupling to ₣A_

Cutover work from ₣A_ informing `rbw-tP`'s sequence has landed (BBAAM depot-identity-collapse). ₣A_'s remaining paces run independently of BB.

## Locked design decisions

- **Depot identity collapse**: RBRR collapses to `RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER`; `RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX` flows through depot-affiliated resources; per-moniker depot fact-files; pristine-fixture moniker autodetect lives in Rust (`rbtdrp_pristine.rs`), not payor.
- **Cult-verb naming**: SA domain muster→**roster**; image domain muster→**audit**. Domain-exclusive split; lowercase tail letters preserved for no-cloud-change observation colophons.
- **Build/run split**: handbook walks cover build only; per-crucible fixtures stay reusable as direct tests.

## Calibrant fixture family — BBAAh

Synthetic Rust fixtures with deterministic verdicts, registered in the manifest by BBAAh. The family exists to give ₣BC `rbk-mvp-3-theurge-self-test` deterministic targets for bash-blackbox driving and manifest-coverage validation; here in ₣BB only the foundation lands.

**Anchor word: `calibrant`.** Module `rbtdrl_calibrant.rs` (classifier `l` for caLibrant since `c` is owned by crucible). Replaces the dormant `rbtdrd_dummy.rs` placeholder. Test counterpart `rbtdtl_calibrant.rs`. Internal-only vocabulary; not end-user-facing.

**Fixture catalog** (Rust, manifest-registered):

- `calibrant-verdicts` (Independent) — pass / fail / skip / pass_with_output. Verdict-path coverage. The case-written file (`output.txt`) is distinct from the engine-auto `trace.txt`.
- `calibrant-fail-fast` (Independent, multi-section) — fixture-internal fail-fast across cases and across sections; "not_reached" cases write sentinel files used as absence-of-side-effect assertions.
- `calibrant-progressing` (StateProgressing) — exercises `rbtdrb_Probe` diagnostic format and `rbtdre_resolve_fail_fast` policy gate.
- `calibrant-sentinel` (Independent) — single case writing a known file when it runs. Used for suite-level fail-fast verification.

Suite: `calibrant` = [verdicts, fail-fast, sentinel]. Sentinel-after-fail verifies cross-fixture fail-fast contract.

Downstream consumption — bash testbench, manifest-coverage runtime checks, coverage-validation fixtures and driver — lives in ₣BC.

## References

- ₣A_ rbk-mvp-3-resource-prefix-and-depot-regen — surfaced the gap
- ₣BC rbk-mvp-3-theurge-self-test — calibrant downstream consumers
- `tt/rbw-tr.QualifyRelease.sh` — current release qualify; layered alongside, not replaced
- `Tools/rbk/rblm_cli.sh` — `rbw-MZ` zeroes local regime; marshal-zero signature baked here per BBAAB
- `.claude/commands/rbk-prep-release.md` — upstream contribution ceremony; pristine-pass becomes a precondition (BBAAH)

# jjezs_pace ₢BBAA1

## Character

Tabtarget-surface refactor. Mechanical except for one judgment call:
the colophon name and channel.

## Goals

Replace the imprint-channel `rbtd-s.FixtureCase.<fixture>.sh [case]`
pattern with a single non-imprint, two-param colophon: param1 is the
fixture name, param2 is the rust test case function name (e.g.
`rbtdrf_rv_rbrv_all_vessels`). The new tabtarget covers ALL
manifest-registered fixtures, not just crucible ones.

The tabtarget is self-documenting — invocation with missing or invalid
arguments fails with a usage listing instead of an opaque error:

- No params: list all valid param1 values (fixture names from the
  manifest); exit nonzero.
- Invalid param1: same — list all valid param1 values; exit nonzero.
- Valid param1, missing or invalid param2: list valid param2 values
  (case names registered for that fixture); exit nonzero.
- Both valid: run the case.

## Wrap criterion

- Single new tabtarget routes by fixture-name lookup against the
  rbtd manifest; rejects unknown fixture names.
- Helpful-failure UX: missing/invalid args produce a list of valid
  values for the offending position and a nonzero exit.
- Per-fixture FixtureCase.<fixture>.sh files retired.
- Crucible-fixture single-case semantics preserved: still operates on
  already-charged state, never charges/quenches.
- BUK channel discipline honored — the new colophon enrolls under the
  param1 channel, not imprint; zipper updated; CLAUDE.md tabtarget table
  reflects the change.
- Fast suite green; canonical onboarding-sequence cases unchanged.

## Out of scope

- FixtureRun (whole-fixture) tabtargets — unchanged.
- Charge/quench tabtargets — unchanged.
- Adding new fixtures or new manifest fields.

## References

- Tools/buk/buk-claude-context.md — channel semantics (imprint vs param1)
